coredata.dc:title: Fe-C microelectrolysis and magnetic field aided interfacial solar driven water evaporation for efficient volatile pollutants removal from water
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yu
Shi, Ruidong
Li, Ning
Liu, Lei
Chang, Qing
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
Xue, Chaorui
coredata.dc:description: Water pollution poses a considerable threat to human life security. In this paper, through the facile spray-coating approach to prepare Fe-C filler/carbon dots/polydopamine composite (Fe-C/CDs/PDA), the magnetic field and microelectrolysis assisted interfacial solar-driven water evaporation (ISDWE) strategy, which is capable of efficiently removing volatile organic chemicals (VOCs) from water, was initially disclosed. Compared with bare Fe-C filler, the water evaporation rate and phenol removal ratio increased to 1.19 kg•m−2•h−1 and 75.40 % under 1.0 kW•m−2 solar irradiation for Fe-C/CDs/PDA. The enhanced solar absorptivity, reduced water evaporation enthalpy, and high wettability achieved by the CDs/PDA coating contributed to the accelerated water evaporation, and the photo-Fenton process induced by the photocatalysis of the CDs/PDA coating played a significant role in improving the phenol removal ratio. Furthermore, the application of a magnetic field led to enhanced water purification performance as accelerated Fe corrosion generated more Fe2+, facilitating water evaporation and promoting microelectrolysis reactions. Finally, Fe-C/CDs/PDA also exhibited high purification performance for other volatile chemicals. A relatively high phenol removal efficiency was likewise achieved by regenerating the Fe-C/CDs/PDA composite. It is expected that our magnetic field-microelectrolysis assisted ISDWE approach will lay the foundation for the design of advanced solar water purification systems in future research undertakings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interfacial solar driven water evaporation
Fe-C microelectrolysis
Volatile organic chemicals
Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
Magnetic field

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52072202 ), China; Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province ( 202403021211219 , 20210302123037 , 201801D221367 ), China; Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, China ; Special fund for Science and Technology Innovation Teams of Shanxi Province ( 202304051001014 ), China; Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, China ; Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province ( 201903D421082 , 201901D111170 ), China; Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), China .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic synthesis process of Fe-C/CDs/PDA. (b) Schematic representation of the ISDWE experiments designed to quantify both the water evaporation rate and phenol removal ratio.
(a-h) SEM images, (i) XRD patterns, (j) survey XPS spectra, and (k) high-resolution Fe2p XPS spectra of Fe-C, Fe-C/PDA, Fe-C/CDs, and Fe-C/CDs/PDA.
(a) water evaporation rates and (b) phenol removal ratio of Fe-C, Fe-C/PDA, Fe-C/CDs and Fe-C/CDs/PDA under different auxiliary magnetic fields of 0 Gs, 27.9 Gs and 62.6 Gs.

(a) UV-Vis-NIR absorption spectra, (b) surface temperature-time profiles under solar irradiance of 1.0 kW•m−2, (c) DSC curves, and (d-g) water transport performances for the samples of Fe-C, Fe-C/CDs, Fe-C/PDA, and Fe-C/CDs/PDA.

(a) Evaporation rates of ultrapure water with varying Fe2+ concentrations in the absence of light. (b) Raman spectra of ultrapure water with varying Fe2+ concentrations. (c) Quantification of Fe ion release for the sample of Fe-C filler under different magnetic fields. (d) Evaporation rates of ultrapure water containing 0.06 mol•L−1 Fe2+ in darkness along with different magnetic fields. (e) Calculation of hydrogen bond energy in water molecules before and after addition of Fe2+, spin and magnetic moment. (Experimental details for a-e were shown in supporting information).

(a) Phenol removal ratio of distilled water for the sample of Fe-C/CDs/PDA with the addition of different amounts of isopropyl alcohol. (b) The absorption spectra of distilled water obtained using ultrapure water as source water for various samples. (c) The absorption spectra of quartz plate coated with CDs, PDA, and CDs/PDA. (d) The tauc plot of CDs, PDA, and CDs/PDA obtained from (c). (e-f) The EIS spectra and photocurrent response of PDA and CDs/PDA. (Experimental details for a-f are shown in supporting information).

(a) Mechanistic illustration of phenol degradation using Fe-C/CDs/PDA under solar irradiation. (b) Cycling and regeneration test for phenol removal ratio and water evaporation rate by Fe-C/CDs/PDA, with cycle test lasting 40 h under solar irradiation at 1.0 kW•m−2. (c) Evaluation of the removal efficiency of various electrochemical catalysts on Fe-C/CDs/PDA under solar irradiation at 1.0 kW•m−2.

head.ce:title: Fe-C microelectrolysis and magnetic field aided interfacial solar driven water evaporation for efficient volatile pollutants removal from water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, State Key Laboratory of Coal and CBM Co-Mining, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, State Key Laboratory of Coal and CBM Co-Mining, North University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, State Key Laboratory of Coal and CBM Co-Mining, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, P R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, P R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution poses a considerable threat to human life security. In this paper, through the facile spray-coating approach to prepare Fe-C filler/carbon dots/polydopamine composite (Fe-C/CDs/PDA), the magnetic field and microelectrolysis assisted interfacial solar-driven water evaporation (ISDWE) strategy, which is capable of efficiently removing volatile organic chemicals (VOCs) from water, was initially disclosed. Compared with bare Fe-C filler, the water evaporation rate and phenol removal ratio increased to 1.19 kg•m−2•h−1 and 75.40 % under 1.0 kW•m−2 solar irradiation for Fe-C/CDs/PDA. The enhanced solar absorptivity, reduced water evaporation enthalpy, and high wettability achieved by the CDs/PDA coating contributed to the accelerated water evaporation, and the photo-Fenton process induced by the photocatalysis of the CDs/PDA coating played a significant role in improving the phenol removal ratio. Furthermore, the application of a magnetic field led to enhanced water purification performance as accelerated Fe corrosion generated more Fe2+, facilitating water evaporation and promoting microelectrolysis reactions. Finally, Fe-C/CDs/PDA also exhibited high purification performance for other volatile chemicals. A relatively high phenol removal efficiency was likewise achieved by regenerating the Fe-C/CDs/PDA composite. It is expected that our magnetic field-microelectrolysis assisted ISDWE approach will lay the foundation for the design of advanced solar water purification systems in future research undertakings.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the global energy demand and severity of environmental issues are continuously growing, many low-carbon and environment-friendly devices based on photothermal, photocatalysis, photovoltaic, triboelectric, and electrochemical energy storage principles have been developed [1–4]. The conventional solar evaporation system, utilizing bottom heating and integral heating, achieves an evaporation efficiency of less than 40 % due to significant heat loss caused by the heating of the entire water body [5]. To this problem, there has been a growing focus on interfacial solar-driven water evaporation technology (ISDWE) as a means to enhance evaporation efficiency. The ISDWE system concentrates the photothermal conversion process at the air-water interface, effectively minimizing heat loss and improving water evaporation efficiency by directly utilizing converted heat to warm surface water [6]. With escalating concerns regarding water pollution and its impact on sustainable development, it is imperative to address these issues through enhanced purification and utilization of polluted water resources. Given its high evaporation efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and low carbon emissions, ISDWE technology has gained widespread application in treating polluted water. Numerous reports have demonstrated the efficient removal of pollutants such as heavy metal ions, organic dyes, volatile organic chemicals (VOCs), bacteria, pesticides, and suspended particles from water using ISDWE technology [7–10]. For instance, in the case of difficult-to-treat volatile organic chemicals (VOCs), a range of strategies and materials have been developed to prevent the accumulation of VOCs in distilled water. An important approach involves the in-situ degradation of VOCs during the ISDWE process. Solar evaporators with exceptional photocatalytic or photo-Fenton performance have been designed to facilitate sustainable water purification [11]. Another strategy entails the sieving-assisted removal of VOCs from distilled water. Peng et al. fabricated a metal-organic framework/polyaniline nanofiber array composite photothermal membrane for effectively removing high-concentration VOCs from water, achieving a 99.0 % rejection ratio for VOCs [12]. Additionally, Wei et al. prepared a polyaniline/polyamide membrane for eliminating VOCs from water, leveraging the molecular sieving property of polyamide to achieve a phenol rejection ratio of 97.0 % [13]. Furthermore, by coupling with efficient adsorption capability, significant removal of VOCs was also achieved during the ISDWE process [14,15].

Microelectrolysis technology, as an efficient, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly water treatment method, has been extensively utilized for the effective remediation of diverse types of wastewater such as industrial effluents and contaminated groundwater. The process triggers galvanic cell action, active species generation, electroflocculation, and electroadsorption effects, resulting in the efficient removal of various pollutants including heavy metals, dyes, halogenated hydrocarbons, volatile phenols, and nitroaromatic hydrocarbons from wastewater [16]. In the case of Fe-C microelectrolysis, micro- and nano-scale zero-valent iron are widely employed due to their high surface area, exceptional surface adsorption capacity and superior chemical reactivity. This utilization significantly enhances the performance of Fe-C microelectrolysis for treating a wide range of pollutants. Meng et al. have fabricated Fe-C microelectrolysis composites by encapsulating mZVI (micro zero-valent iron) and activated carbon particles in porous calcium alginate. The findings indicate a significant enhancement in the utilization and reactivity of mZVI, attributed to its high dispersion within the composite [17]. Furthermore, microelectrolysis has been extensively integrated with various processes such as Fenton oxidation, biological treatment, and physico-chemical treatment to enhance the efficacy of wastewater treatment. Sun et al. synthesized a series of novel Fe-Cu doped alumina catalysts using a facile sol-gel method. During the Fenton reaction process, Cu facilitates the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, leading to increased generation of hydroxyl radicals, resulting in complete removal (100 %) of NB (100 mg•L−1) by the 5Fe2.5Cu-Al2O3 catalyst [18]. Additionally, Zhao et al. reported on a synergistic effect between micro-electrolysis and liquid-phase catalytic electrolysis for gas purification. It was observed that incorporation of Fe/C particle electrodes enhances sustainability and electrical conductivity in electrochemical liquid-phase catalysis, leading to significantly improved removal stability for PH3, H2S, and HCN gases with maintenance of removal efficiencies above 90 % for PH3, H2S, and HCN over an 800-min period (removal efficiencies at 800 min: 90 %, 98 %, 93.68 %, respectively) [19]. Meanwhile, a variety of carbon materials (such as biochar, activated carbon, graphene, etc.) have demonstrated their suitability as ideal candidates for Fe-C micro-electrolysis [20,21]. The preparation of these carbon materials benefits from the abundance of carbon sources and the simplicity and diversity of experimental processes. This underscores the economic advantage of employing Fe-C microelectrolysis for wastewater treatment. Consequently, the development of advanced materials with exceptional microelectrolysis properties holds significant promise for enhancing wastewater purification efforts.

In this study, the focus is on preventing the enrichment of VOCs in distilled water during the ISDWE process. Different from the commonly used methods including photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, adsorption and sieving, through preparing Fe-C microelectrolysis fillers loaded with carbon dots-polydopamine (Fe-C/CDs/PDA), a magnetic field and microelectrolysis coupling method was reported in this paper to efficiently remove VOCs from distilled water during ISDWE process. By employing a simple spraying approach, CDs and PDA were subsequently coated on the surface of the Fe-C filler. The CDs decoration resulted in the improved photothermal ability, the subsequent PDA film coating further improved the wettability and robustness of the surface coating, and the photo-Fenton reactions was also induced by the CDs/PDA composites. The prepared Fe-C/CDs/PDA exhibited enhanced photothermal and microelectrolysis properties compared to bare Fe-C. Under solar irradiation of 1.0 kW•m−2, water evaporation rate of 1.19 kg•m−2•h−1 and phenol removal ratio of 75.40 % in distilled water was achieved, and it was observed that the magnetic field further promoted water evaporation rate and phenol removal efficiency to 1.30 kg•m−2•h−1 and 81.9 %. A series of experiments and characterizations were conducted to investigate the mechanism of magnetic field and microelectrolysis in strengthening the ISDWE performance of Fe-C/CDs/PDA. Computational simulation, which can provide deep understanding on the structure-property relationship, was also carried out using first-principles calculations [22–24]. This work shows promise for providing alternative pathways for refractory wastewater purification using ISDWE technology.

Fe-C filler (Fe-C; diameter 4 cm; iron content: 70 %) was purchased from Jiangsu Zhikang Carbon Industry Technology Co., Ltd. Coal tar pitch was obtained from Hengshui Zehao Rubber Chemical Co., Ltd. Phenol (purity: AR) and sodium hydroxide (purity: 96 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Dopamine (purity: 98 %) was acquired from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium periodate (purity: AR, 99.5 %) from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., and Tris buffer solution (purity: >99.8 %) from Shanghai Blue Season Technology Development Co., Ltd. Nonwoven fabric was purchased from Anmus Technology Co., Ltd. Formic acid, anhydrous ethanol, and hydrogen peroxide (purity: AR) were obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Fe-C/CDs, Fe-C/PDA, and Fe-C/CDs/PDA
ce:section.ce:para: The spray-coating method was used to prepare Fe-C/CDs, Fe-C/PDA, and Fe-C/CDs/PDA ( Fig. 1a). Before the preparation, Fe-C filler was initially sonicated in 200 mL ultrapure water for 10 s, followed by drying in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h. After being prepared using the coal pitch etching method shown in supporting information, carbon dots of 100 mg were dispersed in 10 mL anhydrous ethanol with stirring and sonication to obtain a homogeneous CDs dispersion. According to the reports on fast coating of PDA [25], DA of 5.05 g was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water to obtain solution A, 8.08 g Tris buffer solution (200 × 10−3 M), 0.08 g NaOH solution (6 × 10−3 M), 11.40 g NaIO4 solution (160 × 10−3 M), and 10 mL ultrapure water were mixed to obtain solution B.

To prepare Fe-C/CDs, various volumes of CDs dispersion (1.0 mL, 2.0 mL, 3.0 mL, 4.0 mL, and 5.0 mL) were initially sprayed onto the upper surface of Fe-C filler, followed by vacuum drying at 80 °C for 2 h. The resulting samples were designated as Fe-C/CDs. Spray bottle with a nozzle diameter of 0.3 mm was used to coat CDs on Fe-C fillers, the spraying distance between nozzle and Fe-C filler was kept at 5 cm, and each spray quantity was controlled at 1.0 mL.

To prepare Fe-C/PDA, 0.2 mL solution A and 0.2 mL solution B were simultaneously sprayed onto the upper surface of Fe-C filler, followed by vacuum drying at 80 °C for 2.0 h. The resulting samples were designated as Fe-C/PDA. Two spray bottles with a shared nozzle (diameter: 0.3 mm) was used to coat PDA on Fe-C fillers, the spraying distance between nozzle and Fe-C filler was kept at 5 cm, and each spray quantity was controlled at 0.2 mL.

To prepare Fe-C/CDs/PDA, similar to the preparation of Fe-C/PDA, 0.2 mL solution A and 0.2 mL solution B were simultaneously sprayed onto the upper surface of Fe-C/CDs, followed by vacuum drying at 80 °C for 2.0 h. The resulting samples were designated as Fe-C/CDs/PDA.

The ISDWE experiment was carried out using a custom-built laboratory device (Fig. 1b, S1). Prior to the ISDWE experiment, Fe-C filler was placed onto a polystyrene support within a glass container, with the lower surface of the Fe-C filler being enveloped by a layer of nonwoven fabric to ensure sufficient water supply. In instances where magnetic field assistance was employed, 6 or 12 magnets (Φ=15 mm) were centrosymmetrically embedded within polystyrene foam and placed 1.0 cm beneath the Fe-C filler (Fig. 1b). The magnetic field strengths were determined to be 27.9 Gs and 62.6 Gs at a position 1 cm above the center of the 6- and 12-magnets ring. A cotton thread was employed to facilitate water pumping from below to the Fe-C filler. To minimize thermal losses, the glass container was insulated with polystyrene foam.

During the ISDWE experiment, 40 mL of ultrapure water was injected into the glass container, which was placed on a high-precision electronic balance (EX223ZH, OHAUS) to monitor the water loss. A Xenon lamp (CEL-HXUV300, CEAULIGHT) was utilized to provide simulated solar irradiance for 1.5 h while maintaining the light intensity at 1.0 kW•m−2 on the sample surface. The initial 30 min of solar irradiance were allocated for stabilizing the temperature of Fe-C filler. Subsequently, over a period of 60 min, water loss was monitored every 5 min and the data obtained was used to calculate the rate of water evaporation.

In order to assess the efficacy of phenol removal, solutions with varying concentrations of phenol were utilized in lieu of ultrapure water, and a condensing cover was used to collect the distilled water (Fig. 1b). The concentration of phenol in both distilled and undistilled water was determined using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600) at an absorption wavelength of 270 nm. The calibration curve depicting absorbance as a function of phenol concentration is presented in Figure S2.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a D8 ADVANCE diffractometer operated at 40 kV and 30 mA with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). The morphology of the samples was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Sigma 300). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS system with Al Kα irradiation (1486.6 eV), and the binding energies were corrected using the C1s peak at 284.8 eV. Light absorbance spectra were acquired using a UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (UH4150) equipped with an integrating sphere assembly. The water evaporation enthalpy was determined using a HSC-2 differential scanning calorimeter within a temperature range of −5 to 200 °C and a heating rate of 10 °C⋅min−1. Raman spectra were measured using a Raman spectrometer (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution). Electrochemical data were recorded and analyzed using the SP-200 Bio-logic electrochemical workstation. Inductively coupled plasma tests (ICP) were conducted using a ICP-OES 5110 system. FT-IR spectra were tested using the Nicolet iS20 spectrometer. Electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) was obtained on Bruker EMXplus-6/1 under solar irradiation of 1.0 kW•m−2. The surface area and pore size distribution of Fe-C filler was measured using mercury intrusion method on Micromeritics AutoPore V 9620. The surface area of carbon dots was characterized by N2 adsorption-desorption analysis at 77 K using a quantachrome instrument (Micromeritics ASAP 2460), and the degassing temperature was 60 °C.

All calculations were carried out for the material in the framework of Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP 6.3.0). The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) function was used to describe the exchange-correlation energy [26]. The projected augmented wave (PAW) method and pseudopotentials were used to describe the interactions between valence electrons and ions [27]. To ensure the efficiency of the computational results and parallel computing, A 7*7*5 k-point grid under Monkhorst-Pack was used in the optimization process and 500 eV truncation energy was set. The lattice parameters and ionic positions of all crystals were fully relaxed, and the convergence criteria for the total energy of all relaxed atoms and the final force were 10−6 eV and 0.01 eV/Å, respectively.

The ISDWE-microelectrolysis coupling evaporator was fabricated by sequentially applying CDs and PDA onto the surface of commercial Fe-C filler using the spraying method. Figure S3 depicts the optical photographs of the bare Fe-C, Fe-C/CDs, and Fe-C/CDs/PDA. It is evident that the color of the Fe-C filler undergoes a transition from cyan to brown and black after the spray-coating process. The SEM image in Fig. 2a and 2e illustrates that the surface of bare Fe-C particle is relatively rough, with a "coral"-like structure consisting of many needles clearly identifiable (blue dotted area). The application of CDs and PDA results in a more homogeneous surface, eliminating the needle-constructed "coral"-like structure (Fig. 2b-2d, Fig. 2f-2h). Particularly noteworthy is that as shown in Fig. 2d and 2h, the sample of Fe-C/CDs/PDA exhibited a highly uniform-coating surface. This could be attributed to an increased degree of polymerization of DA molecule induced by carbon dots. Amino group-related reactions between CDs and PDA may promote the polymerization of DA molecule [28]. Furthermore, a substantial number of nano and micro-sized pores can be identified in bare Fe-C filler (Figure S4), through CDs and PDA coating, the obstruction of many nanopores (indicated by the red arrow) on the surface of Fe-C filler was observed, while the micro-size porous channels remained intact (indicated by yellow arrow). This can ensure efficient water transport to the evaporation region for Fe-C/CDs, Fe-C/PDA, and Fe-C/CDs/PDA.

The compositional analysis from XRD and XPS further confirms the successful application of CDs and PDA onto the surface of Fe-C particles. The XRD patterns (Fig. 2i) of Fe-C/CDs, Fe-C/PDA, and Fe-C/CDs/PDA exhibited similarity to that of bare Fe-C. XRD diffraction peaks, which correspond to Fe2O3, Fe, and carbon, can all be identified for all samples, indicating preservation of the crystal structure of Fe-C filler. Herein, the XRD pattern of carbon dots shows no diffraction peak in Figure S5, suggesting its amorphous structure. Thus, the existence of carbon dots cannot be determined from the XRD pattern. Fig. 2j and 2k are the XPS spectra of as-prepared samples. Firstly, survey XPS spectra in Fig. 2j revealed a higher intensity N1s peak for Fe-C/PDA and Fe-C/CDs/PDA compared to bare Fe-C and Fe-C/CDs, indicating the PDA coating on the surface. The high-resolution C1s and N1s spectra of the four samples was also characterized and shown in Figures S6 and S7, both Fe-C/PDA and Fe-C/CDs/PDA exhibit a subpeak of C-N (285.5 eV) in the C1s XPS spectra, and three N1s subpeaks positioned at 398.4 eV (-NH2), 400.1 eV (-NH-), and 401.8 eV (-N = ), thereby confirming the polymerization of PDA on the sample surface. In accordance with the polymerization mechanism of PDA proposed by Hong et al., compared with Fe-C/PDA, the elevated content of -NH- in Fe-C/CDs/PDA can affirm the high degree of polymerization of PDA by carbon dots [29]. Secondly, in Fig. 2k, distinct peaks corresponding to Fe2p were observed for Fe-C, Fe-C/CDs, and Fe-C/PDA, while no such peak was present for Fe-C/CDs/PDA. This observation suggests uniform and dense coverage of CDs/PDA on the surface of Fe-C particles. It can also be noted that the Fe2p XPS peak is predominantly located at 711.0 eV, corresponding to iron oxides [30]. This can be attributed to the partial oxidation of Fe in Fe-C fillers, which is also evidenced in the XRD patterns of Fig. 2i. Based on the abovementioned discussion, it can be concluded that CDs and PDA were uniformly adorned on the surface of the Fe-C filler for the sample of Fe-C/CDs/PDA. Notably, due to the recombination of PDA, the shedding of CDs can be effectively prevented. As shown in Figure S8, the water soaked with Fe-C/CDs exhibited a robust fluorescence peak of CDs, whereas the peak intensity was notably diminished for Fe-C/CDs/PDA.

The water evaporation and phenol removal efficiency of various sample were assessed with and without magnetic fields, the obtained results were shown in Fig. 3 and Table S1. The results revealed that Fe-C/PDA and Fe-C/CDs exhibited water evaporation rates of 0.97 kg•m−2•h−1 and 1.23 kg•m−2•h−1, which are 1.17 times and 1.48 times higher than that of bare Fe-C (0.83 kg•m−2•h−1). When coating CDs/PDA on the surface of Fe-C filler, the water evaporation rate for Fe-C/CDs/PDA was 1.19 kg•m−2•h−1, which is also significantly higher than that of bare Fe-C (Fig. 3a). The dense PDA layer in Fe-C/CDs/PDA might reduce the surface evaporation area, and cause the slight reduction of water evaporation rate compared to Fe-C/CDs. Furthermore, it was observed that the water evaporation rate of Fe-C/CDs/PDA was enhanced by the application of a magnetic field, reaching 1.25 kg•m−2•h−1 and 1.30 kg•m−2•h−1 at specific magnetic field strengths of 27.9 Gs and 62.6 Gs (Fig. 3a). Similarly, the water evaporation rate of Fe-C/CDs was also enhanced by the application of a magnetic field. As depicted in Figure S9, the water evaporation rate of Fe-C/CDs rose from 1.23 kg•m−2•h−1 under 0 Gs to 1.30 kg•m−2•h−1 under 62.6 Gs. These results indicate the active role of CDs, PDA, and magnetic field in improving the ISDWE performance of Fe-C fillers. The same trend was observed for the phenol removal efficiency. As shown in Fig. 3b, Fe-C/CDs and Fe-C/PDA demonstrated increased phenol removal ratios of 72.95 % and 74.20 %, and the highest removal ratio achieved was 81.90 % for Fe-C/CDs/PDA under a specific magnetic field strength of 62.6 Gs - significantly surpassing the performance of bare Fe-C (69.28 %). In one word, the ISDWE-microelectrolysis coupling system, which was built and optimized through preparing Fe-C/CDs/PDA composite, demonstrated excellent water purification performance, the water evaporation rate and phenol removal ratio were further improved through applying a magnetic field. The detailed mechanism for the improved ISDWE-microelectrolysis coupling effect is detailed as follows.

UV-Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was employed to investigate the light absorption characteristics of various samples, with the corresponding results depicted in Fig. 4a. It is evident that, different from bare Fe-C, Fe-C/CDs display intensified absorbance at ∼300 nm, whereas Fe-C/PDA and Fe-C/CDs/PDA exhibit another intensified absorbance in the Vis-NIR region. This discrepancy is likely attributed to the presence of CDs and PDA on the surface. π-π* transition of the aromatic ring might be responsible for the absorption peak at ∼300 nm [31,32], meanwhile the strong π-π interactions on PDA can help improve the absorption capacity in the Vis-NIR region [33]. The SEM findings have affirmed that CDs can provide more nucleation sites to facilitate the polymerization of DA molecules, resulting in highly polymerized PDA in Fe-C/CDs/PDA as opposed to Fe-C/PDA, and thereby further augmenting its light absorption capacity [34]. The improved light absorption ability is conducive to high photothermal performance. The temperature-time curves depicted in Fig. 4b provide compelling evidence to support the superior photothermal conversion capability of Fe-C/CDs/PDA. Under a solar irradiance of 1.0 kW•m−2 over a period of 3600 s, the steady surface temperature of Fe-C/CDs/PDA reached 58.0 °C, surpassing that of Fe-C/CDs (57.5 °C), Fe-C/PDA (56.0 °C), and Fe-C (55.5 °C).

The DSC analysis results for the various samples are presented in Fig. 4c, with the water evaporation enthalpy values calculated from the DSC curves shown in the inset table. It is evident that the water evaporation enthalpy follows a descending order: pure water (2609.8 kJ•kg−1), Fe-C (1339.5 kJ•kg−1), and Fe-C/CDs/PDA (1127.9 kJ•kg−1). This indicates a reduced heat requirement for water evaporation on Fe-C/CDs/PDA, which is conducive to achieving high water evaporation rate. The presence of abundant hydroxyl, amino, and aldehyde groups on the PDA surface contributes to its hydrophilic nature, thereby reducing thermal resistance at the solid-liquid interface and leading to lower water evaporation enthalpy [35].

By employing a simple water-transport test, the water wettability of various samples can be assessed, and the obtained results are shown in Fig. 4d-4g. It is observed that the water diffusion distance for Fe-C/CDs is 3.55 cm, which is significantly lower than Fe-C (4.25 cm), Fe-C/PDA (4.15 cm), and Fe-C/CDs/PDA (4.10 cm). This indicates that PDA, rich in hydrophilic groups, contributes to enhancing water wettability, while CDs may have a detrimental effect on water wettability. As depicted in Figure S10, the water wettability of Fe-C/CDs decreased with an increase in CDs load, leading to a reduction in the rate of water evaporation. The small-sized CDs not only have a high surface area of 439 m2•g−1, but the surface groups also comprise hydrophobic -CHx (Figure S11), thus the coating of CDs will exert adverse effects on the surface wettability [36,37]. In the process of ISDWE, high water wettability plays a crucial role in ensuring sufficient water supply and facilitating rapid evaporation. However, for Fe-C/CDs/PDA, the dense PDA layer on the surface may impede the escape of water vapor, resulting in a lower water evaporation rate of 1.19 kg•m−2•h−1 compared to Fe-C/CDs (1.23 kg•m−2•h−1). Based on the aforementioned results, the application of CDs/PDA coating was conducive to enhanced photothermal conversion efficiency, sufficient water retention, and decreased water evaporation enthalpy. Consequently, this resulted in the improved ISDWE performance of Fe-C/CDs/PDA.

Subsequently, the auxiliary effect of the magnetic field was further elucidated through a combination of experimental data and DFT calculations. The evaporation rates of purified water with Fe2+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 0.1 mol•L−1 were measured and are presented in Fig. 5a. It was observed that the addition of Fe2+ led to improved water evaporation rates, with a maximum rate of 0.01677 kg•m−2•h−1 achieved at a Fe2+ concentration of 0.06 mol•L−1. This enhancement can be attributed to the disruption of hydrogen bonds by the presence of Fe2+. Fig. 5b displays Raman spectra for Fe2+ aqueous solutions at different concentrations, revealing a gradual decrease in intensity for the shoulder peak at ∼3205 cm−1 as the Fe2+ concentration increases. As indicated in the reference, the peaks at approximately 3205 cm−1, 3400 cm−1, and 3600 cm−1 respectively correspond to completely hydrogen-bonded water molecules (C-water), partially hydrogen-bonded water molecules (P-water), and free water molecules (F-water). Thus, the Raman spectra were deconvoluted and presented in Figure S12. It is calculated that the content of C-water decreased from 33.41 % in pure water to 29.78 % in the 0.10 mol•L−1 Fe2+ solution. Therefore, the attenuated Raman peak at 3205 cm−1 confirms the disruptive effect of Fe2+ on the hydrogen bonds between water molecules. Fig. 5c illustrates the impact of Fe-C particles and a magnetic field on the concentration of Fe ions in ultrapure water. It is evident that, with the introduction of Fe-C particles and a magnetic field, the concentration of Fe ions increased from 0.81 × 10−7 mol•L−1 to 1.74 × 10−7 mol•L−1, indicating the release of Fe ions from Fe-C particles and enhancement by the magnetic field. The corrosion of metallic Fe during the micro-electrolysis process may account for the abundance of Fe ions in purified water. Furthermore, it has been documented that a magnetic field can decrease the corrosion potential of Fe-C material [38], leading to accelerated release of Fe ions. Therefore, both spontaneous Fe2+ release from Fe-C and accelerated corrosion induced by magnetic field likely play crucial roles in improving water evaporation.

The evaporation rate of Fe2+ aqueous solution was measured under varying magnetic fields and in dark environment. As depicted in Fig. 5d, the water evaporation rate exhibited a consistent increase with the rise in magnetic field strength from 300.9 Gs to 508.5 Gs. This demonstrates the ability of magnetic field to enhance the water evaporation of Fe2+ aqueous solution. Computational simulation further supports the capacity of Fe2+ and magnetic fields to reduce the hydrogen bond energy between water molecules. As depicted in Fig. 5e, upon the addition of Fe2+ and magnetic moment, the hydrogen bond energy of water molecules amounts to 0.288 eV, in contrast to 0.123 eV when neither Fe2+ nor magnetic moment is added and −0.046 eV when only Fe2+ is added. A lower hydrogen bond energy implies a stronger bonding between water molecules. The calculations suggest that the simultaneous application of Fe2+ and magnetic moment can expedite the motion between water molecules, thereby facilitating the breakage of hydrogen bonds. Herein, a stronger bonding between water molecules can also be discerned upon the addition of only Fe2+, and the possible reason might be the hydration effect of Fe2+ [39]. Since the process of water evaporation primarily involves breaking hydrogen bonds between water molecules, numerical simulations provide additional evidence for enhanced ISDWE performance of Fe-C/CDs/PDA under a magnetic field.

In order to gain a comprehensive understanding of the enhanced phenol removal performance, a series of additional experiments were conducted, and the results are presented in Fig. 6. Initially, considering the potential involvement of the Fenton process in phenol degradation, the phenol removal efficiency of Fe-C/CDs/PDA was evaluated with the addition of isopropyl alcohol as •OH scavengers in the phenol aqueous solution. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, the inclusion of isopropyl alcohol led to a significant reduction in the phenol removal ratio from 79.30 % to 64.95 %. The generation of •OH by Fe-C/CDs/PDA was further confirmed in the ESR spectra of Figure S13. It can be found that, after irradiation under solar light for 10 min, characteristic signals of •OH radicals are detected. Given that •OH radicals are known for their effective ability to degrade phenol due to their strong oxidation capacity, it can be anticipated that the generation of •OH radicals significantly contributed to enhancing the phenol removal ratio, potentially attributed to the photocatalytic property of CDs/PDA coating. Fig. 6b illustrates the absorption spectra of colorimetric-reaction treated distilled water obtained by various samples under ISDWE experiments at 1.0 kW•m−2, and the H2O2 production capabilities of various samples can be compared by measuring the peak intensity at 350 nm. Significantly, the melamine sponge loaded with CDs/PDA (MS/CDs/PDA) demonstrated enhanced photocatalytic H2O2 production ability compared to bare MS, MS/CDs, and MS/PDA. This suggests that, in comparison to bare CDs and PDA coating, CDs/PDA coating exhibits a higher capacity for reducing O2 into H2O2 through the photocatalytic reduction pathway. Consequently, CDs/PDA coating holds promise for in-situ H2O2 production during ISDWE process. As anticipated, Fe-C/CDs/PDA exhibited remarkable performance for in-situ H2O2 production, which is significantly different from bare Fe-C with no photocatalytic H2O2 production ability. Upon contact with H2O2, leached Fe2+ could directly decompose it into •OH radicals [40], subsequently oxidizing phenol due to the high redox potential of •OH/H2O. Therefore, the superior phenol removal capability of Fe-C/CDs/PDA may be attributed to the coupling of photo-Fenton process with microelectrolysis. Furthermore, under magnetic field conditions, increased corrosion of Fe-C could generate more Fe2+ ions, facilitate generation of •OH radicals and further improve phenol removal efficiency.

Control experiments were conducted to elucidate the reasons behind the superior phenol removal capability of Fe-C/CDs/PDA compared to Fe-C/CDs and Fe-C/PDA. Fig. 6c and 6d illustrate the UV-Vis-NIR diffuse reflectance absorption spectra and tauc plots of different coatings. It is evident that, in comparison to bare CDs and PDA coating, the light absorbance of CDs/PDA in the near infrared region is enhanced, indicating a reduced band gap of 0.72 eV. This finding aligns with the results presented in Fig. 4a, and the improved light absorption can be attributed to the high polymerization of DA molecule. The enhanced light absorption of CDs/PDA leads to increased charge generation, which contributes to higher photocatalytic H2O2 production. Additionally, Fig. 6e depicts Nyquist plots of as-prepared PDA and CDs/PDA electrodes. The plots consist of semicircles at high frequencies and straight lines at low frequencies. It is observed that the arc semidiameters decrease for the sample of CDs/PDA, demonstrating reduced electron transfer resistance. Furthermore, incorporation of CDs significantly mitigates electron-hole recombination in PDA, as evidenced by photoluminescence spectroscopy (Figure S13) and photocurrent response (Fig. 6f), where CDs/PDA exhibits lower PL intensity and higher photocurrent than PDA. The improved photoinduced charge carrier separation in CDs/PDA might be attributed to the electron-repositories property of CDs [41]. The recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in PDA can be effectively inhibited by CDs, thereby enhancing the self-electron-donating ability of PDA and achieving a higher H2O2 production rate [42]. Based on the aforementioned findings, the presence of CDs in PDA not only augmented light absorption but also facilitated charge transfer and separation, thereby elucidating the superior efficiency of Fe-C/CDs/PDA for phenol removal compared to Fe-C/CDs and Fe-C/PDA.

Based on the above results, mechanistic understanding of phenol degradation over Fe-C/CDs/PDA was proposed and depicted in Fig. 7a. Firstly, the coating of CDs/PDA enhances photothermal conversion, leading to an increase in filler temperature and subsequent acceleration of water evaporation. Secondly, the introduction of CDs improves the photocatalytic property of PDA, thereby promoting the generation of H2O2 and initiating the photo-Fenton process coupled with microelectrolysis for enhanced phenol degradation. Thirdly, under a magnetic field, the Fe-C filler releases more Fe2+ due to reduced corrosion potential, facilitating both microelectrolysis and water evaporation by reducing hydrogen bonding between water molecules. Consequently, the as-prepared Fe-C/CDs/PDA demonstrated efficient phenol removal in distilled water through coupling ISDWE with microelectrolysis. The depicted chemical reactions below can produce active species of Fe2+, [H], and •OH, which in turn facilitate the degradation of phenol.
(1) Fe - 2e- → Fe2+
(2) 2H+ + 2e- → 2[H]
(3) CDs/PDA + hv → h+ + e-
(4) 2e- + O2 + 2H+ → H2O2
(5) Fe2+ + H2O2 + H+ → Fe3+ + •OH + H2O

After the 10-cycling test for 40 h, the performance degradation was observed (Fig. 7b), the phenol removal ratio decreased from 82.33 % to 53.39 %, and water evaporation rate decreased from 1.30 kg•m−2•h−1 to 0.78 kg•m−2•h−1. The reason could be the passivation and hardening of Fe-C filler [43]. Notably, with immersing HCl solution (0.1 mol•L−1) for 24 h, the phenol removal ratio and water evaporation rate resumed to 76.20 % and 1.12 kg•m−2•h−1, indicating the renewable ability for our Fe-C/CDs/PDA. Herein, the sample of Fe-C/CDs/PDA was immersed in water for 7 days to assess the stability of the CDs/PDA coating. The increased chemical oxygen demand (COD) value of the purified water can be observed in Figure S15, suggesting the potential release of PDA into water. This might lead to the deterioration of performance during long-term usage, and further optimization is required. In Fig. 7c, excellent removal performance is also demonstrated when using other volatile chemicals including aniline, p-chlorophenol, catechol, and o-chlorophenol, with respective removal ratios tested at 82.34 %, 88.94 %, 94.89 %, and 70.40 % respectively; indicating the general applicability of our ISDWE-microelectrolysis coupling strategy.

In summary, we propose a novel water purification approach that integrates interfacial solar-driven water evaporation (ISDWE) with magnetic field and microelectrolysis. Through the modification of Fe-C filler surface with carbon dots and polydopamine, we have significantly enhanced the photothermal conversion capability to improve the ISDWE performance. Under 1.0 kW•m−2 solar irradiation, the water evaporation rate has been boosted from 0.83 kg•m−2•h−1 for bare Fe-C fillers to 1.19 kg•m−2•h−1 for Fe-C/CDs/PDA. Moreover, when a magnetic field is applied, the water evaporation rate further increased to 1.30 kg•m−2•h−1 due to accelerated release of Fe2+ into water by the magnetic field, resulting in reduced hydrogen bonding between water molecules. In treating phenol-polluted water, Fe-C/CDs/PDA achieves an impressive phenol removal ratio of ∼81.9 % under magnetic field of 62.6 Gs. The CDs/PDA layer induces photo-Fenton reactions to facilitate phenol degradation and simultaneously promotes microelectrolysis through releasing more Fe2+ under a magnetic field condition. Additionally, our magnetic field/microelectrolysis coupling ISDWE strategy demonstrates efficient removal performance for other volatile pollutants including aniline, p-chlorophenol, catechol, and o-chlorophenol.

Liu Lei: Methodology, Investigation. Li Ning: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shi Ruidong: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Zhang Yu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Chang Qing: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Hu Shengliang: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Yang Jinlong: Resources, Funding acquisition. Xue Chaorui: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation.
